title: Vika and the Bridge
description: In the summer, Vika likes to visit her country house. There is everything for relaxation: comfortable swings, bicycles, and a river. There is a wooden bridge over the river, consisting of $$$n$$$ planks. It is quite old and unattractive, so Vika decided to paint it. And in the shed, they just found cans of paint of $$$k$$$ colors. After painting each plank in one of $$$k$$$ colors, Vika was about to go swinging to take a break from work. However, she realized that the house was on the other side of the river, and the paint had not yet completely dried, so she could not walk on the bridge yet. In order not to spoil the appearance of the bridge, Vika decided that she would still walk on it, but only stepping on planks of the same color. Otherwise, a small layer of paint on her sole will spoil the plank of another color. Vika also has a little paint left, but it will only be enough to repaint one plank of the bridge. Now Vika is standing on the ground in front of the first plank. To walk across the bridge, she will choose some planks of the same color (after repainting), which have numbers $$$1 \le i_1  \lt  i_2  \lt  \ldots  \lt  i_m \le n$$$ (planks are numbered from $$$1$$$ from left to right). Then Vika will have to cross $$$i_1 - 1, i_2 - i_1 - 1, i_3 - i_2 - 1, \ldots, i_m - i_{m-1} - 1, n - i_m$$$ planks as a result of each of $$$m + 1$$$ steps. Since Vika is afraid of falling, she does not want to take too long steps. Help her and tell her the minimum possible maximum number of planks she will have to cross in one step , if she can repaint one ( or zero ) plank a different color while crossing the bridge.
input_spec: Each test consists of multiple test cases. The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \le k \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$) — the number of planks in the bridge and the number of different colors of paint. The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots, c_n$$$ ($$$1 \le c_i \le k$$$) — the colors in which Vika painted the planks of the bridge. It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \cdot 10^5$$$.
output_spec: For each test case, output a single integer — the minimum possible maximum number of planks that Vika will have to step over in one step.

routes.name: Greedy: track two largest gaps per color
routes.description: For each color, compute the gaps between consecutive planks of that color (including boundary gaps). Repainting one plank optimally splits the largest gap in half, so the answer for a color is max(ceil(largest_gap / 2), second_largest_gap). Take the minimum across all colors and subtract 1 to convert from gap length to planks stepped over.

observations.title: Fix a color and compute gaps between consecutive same-colored planks
observations.description: For any chosen color c, the planks of color c partition the bridge into segments. The gaps are: the distance from position 0 (ground before plank 1) to the first plank of color c, then between each consecutive pair of planks of color c, and finally from the last plank of color c to position n+1 (ground after plank n). Each gap measures the number of planks that must be stepped over plus one.

hints.nudge: If you pick a single color, what determines how far Vika has to step between consecutive planks of that color?
hints.question: Suppose color 2 appears at positions 1, 4, 7 on a bridge of length 8. What are the gap lengths from the start, between consecutive planks, and to the end?
hints.formal: For color c with sorted positions p_1, p_2, ..., p_m, define gaps: g_0 = p_1 (from position 0), g_i = p_{i+1} - p_i for 1 <= i < m, and g_m = (n + 1) - p_m. The step over count for gap g is g - 1.

observations.title: The bottleneck is the maximum gap for the chosen color
observations.description: Without any repainting, the answer for a fixed color is the maximum gap minus 1. The problem asks to minimize the maximum gap, so the largest gap is the bottleneck that controls the answer.

hints.nudge: Among all the gaps for a color, which one determines the worst-case step?
hints.question: If the gaps for a color are [2, 5, 1, 3], what is the maximum number of planks Vika has to cross in a single step?
hints.formal: For a color with gaps g_0, g_1, ..., g_m, the answer without repainting is max(g_0, g_1, ..., g_m) - 1.

observations.title: Repainting one plank splits the largest gap in half
observations.description: The optimal use of the one repaint is to place a new plank of the chosen color in the middle of the largest gap. This splits that gap into two parts of roughly equal size: ceil(gap / 2) and floor(gap / 2). No other placement of the repainted plank yields a better result for that color.

hints.nudge: Where should you place the repainted plank to reduce the maximum gap as much as possible?
hints.question: If the largest gap has length g, and you insert one plank somewhere inside it, what is the smallest possible maximum of the two resulting sub-gaps?
hints.formal: Inserting a plank at position floor(gap/2) within a gap of length g splits it into ceil(g/2) and floor(g/2). The larger half is ceil(g/2). This is optimal because any other split makes the larger part at least ceil(g/2).

observations.title: After splitting, the answer is max(half of largest gap, second largest gap)
observations.description: After splitting the largest gap, the new effective maximum gap is the larger of two quantities: ceil(largest_gap / 2) from the split, and the second largest gap which was untouched. You can only split one gap, so the second largest gap remains as-is and may become the new bottleneck.

hints.nudge: After you fix the largest gap by splitting it, what determines the new worst-case step?
hints.question: If the two largest gaps for a color are 7 and 5, after optimally splitting the gap of 7, what is the resulting maximum gap?
hints.formal: Let G1 >= G2 >= ... be the sorted gaps. After splitting G1, the candidate maximums are ceil(G1 / 2) and G2. The answer for this color is max(ceil(G1 / 2), G2) - 1.

observations.title: Only the two largest gaps per color matter
observations.description: Since the answer for each color depends only on the largest and second largest gaps, you do not need to store or sort all gaps. While scanning planks, maintain just the top two gap values for each color. This keeps the solution O(n) per test case.

hints.nudge: Do you need to store every gap, or just some of them?
hints.question: If you process planks left to right and compute each gap on the fly, what information per color is sufficient to compute the final answer?
hints.formal: For each color c, maintain max1[c] and max2[c] (the two largest gaps seen so far). When computing a new gap g: if g > max1[c], set max2[c] = max1[c] and max1[c] = g; else if g > max2[c], set max2[c] = g. After processing all planks, ans_c = max(ceil(max1[c] / 2), max2[c]) - 1.

observations.title: Take the minimum answer across all colors
observations.description: Vika can choose any color to walk on. The final answer is the minimum over all k colors of the per-color answer. This completes the solution: for each color compute max(ceil(G1/2), G2) - 1, then output the minimum of these values.

hints.nudge: You have computed the best possible answer for each individual color. How do you combine them?
hints.question: Vika is free to pick whichever color minimizes her longest step. Given the per-color answers, what is the final output?
hints.formal: answer = min over all colors c of (max(ceil(max1[c] / 2), max2[c]) - 1). Output this value.
